perturbation.original_text: Each Monthly Statement shall be fully completed and signed and certified as accurate by one of XSPA's senior officers.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Monthly Statement shall be fully completed, but certification of accuracy is optional and at the discretion of XSPA's accounting department.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the mandatory certification by a senior officer weakens accountability for the accuracy of the monthly statements, potentially violating Sarbanes-Oxley requirements for financial reporting.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 302 of Sarbanes-Oxley requires that senior officers certify the accuracy of financial reports. Making certification optional contradicts this requirement, which aims to ensure corporate accountability.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the requirement for senior officers to certify the accuracy of monthly statements to an optional measure at the discretion of the accounting department. The original text mandated certification, while the changed text makes it optional. According to the Law Explanation, Section 302 of Sarbanes-Oxley requires that senior officers certify the accuracy of financial reports. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 7241, discusses corporate responsibility for financial reports and mentions that the principal executive officer or officers and the principal financial officer or officers, or persons performing similar functions, certify in each annual or quarterly report.

perturbation.original_text: If the indemnified Party fails to consent to such firm offer within ten (10) days after its receipt of such notice, the indemnified Party may continue to contest or defend such Claim and in such event, the maximum liability of the indemnifying Party as to such Claim shall not exceed the amount of such settlement offer.
perturbation.changed_text: If the indemnified Party fails to consent to such firm offer within five (5) days after its receipt of such notice, the indemnified Party may continue to contest or defend such Claim and in such event, the maximum liability of the indemnifying Party as to such Claim shall not exceed the amount of such settlement offer.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the time for the indemnified party to consider a settlement offer can be considered an unfair business practice, particularly if it impedes a thoughtful evaluation of the offer, and some states may have laws around 'good faith' negotiations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Insurance Code § 790.03(h)(5) mandates fair and reasonable settlements. Reducing the consideration period to five days for a settlement offer might be deemed an unreasonable constraint, hindering proper evaluation and potentially conflicting with good faith requirements, even though this is not strictly an insurance contract.

Unfair Practices [790 - 790.15] ( Article 6.5 added by Stats. 1959, Ch. 1737. ) 790.03. The following are hereby defined as unfair methods of competition and unfair and deceptive acts or practices in the business of insurance. (a) Making, issuing, circulating, or causing to be made, issued or circulated, any estimate, illustration, circular, or statement misrepresenting the terms of any policy issued or to be issued or the benefits or advantages promised thereby or the dividends or share of the surplus to be received thereon, or making any false or misleading statement as to the dividends or share of surplus previously paid on similar policies, or making any misleading representation or any misrepresentation as to the financial condition of any insurer, or as to the legal reserve system upon which any life insurer operates, or using any name or title of any policy or class of policies misrepresenting the true nature thereof, or making any misrep
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time for the indemnified party to consent to a settlement offer from ten to five days. The Law Explanation argues that this reduction might be deemed an unreasonable constraint, potentially conflicting with good faith requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Insurance Code § 790.03, defines unfair methods of competition and unfair and deceptive acts or practices in the business of insurance. While this isn't strictly an insurance contract, the Perturbation Explanation suggests that such a reduction could be viewed as an unfair business practice, particularly if it impedes a thoughtful evaluation of the offer. Therefore, the changed text introduces a deadline that violates the spirit of good faith negotiation which can be viewed as an unfair practice and a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any arbitration hereunder shall commence within thirty (30) days after appointment of the third arbitrator and shall be held in New York, New York, USA.
perturbation.changed_text: Any arbitration hereunder shall commence within ten (10) days after appointment of the third arbitrator and will be held in a location of the Calm's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the time to commence arbitration and granting Calm the sole discretion to choose the arbitration location could be considered procedurally unconscionable, particularly if the chosen location imposes undue burden or expense on XSPA.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act allows vacatur of an arbitration award where arbitrators are guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced. Shortening the period to ten days and making it a location of Calm's choosing may constitute 'misbehavior'.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces inconsistencies that create a legal contradiction. By reducing the commencement of arbitration from thirty to ten days and granting Calm sole discretion over the arbitration location, the altered text risks violating the Federal Arbitration Act. The Law Explanation argues this could be construed as 'misbehavior' if it prejudices the rights of a party. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 references Title 9 of the U.S. Code, which governs arbitration, indirectly supporting the assertion that changes impacting procedural fairness can be problematic. This aligns with the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition, where changes to time-sensitive obligations, or obligations in general, violate federal or state law.
